(3) Confession of covenant breach as movement back toward God: Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036) — Daniel's prayer is a movement of confessional return toward God after covenant failure — using covenantal language to re-engage the God whose covenant has been broken. (4) Swearing by God's name as allegiance (3308-003 — OBS-034-055): taking an oath in God's name as an act of inner allegiance directed toward God.

Addressed in Q&A-033 (T1.7.3) — the inner-being state is: desolation (Eph 2:12 — OBS-034-060), bondage (Gal 4:24 — OBS-034-071), moral dissolution (Rom 1:31 — OBS-034-057), and curse-activation for those who entered but then rejected the covenant (Dan 9:11 — OBS-034-036; Heb 10:29 — OBS-034-070). Consistent with T1.7.3. No new findings — confirmed stable.

Yes — the evidence shows covenant operating differently depending on prior relational standing. (1) Within existing bonds: covenant deepens and formalises existing relational connections — the Jonathan-David covenant (3304-004 — OBS-034-049) formalises an existing relationship of loyalty; the Sinai covenant structures the already-existent Exodus-community relationship between God and Israel. Within an existing bond, covenant adds formal binding commitment, mutual obligation, and the oath/curse structure to a relationship that already has relational content. (2) Across relational distance: covenant can also initiate a relationship across distance or difference — the Noahic covenant extends to all living creatures (Gen 9:9 — OBS-034-038), including those not in prior relationship with God. Treaty covenants (H1285 1a1 — OBS-034-009) are made between previously separate parties. In these cases, covenant is the initiating structure that creates relationship across prior separation. (3) The prohibited covenant (3304-003 — OBS-034-048) operates across religious/cultural difference — making covenant with Canaanite inhabitants creates an illegitimate relational bond across a boundary that should not be crossed. The boundary-question is structurally present in the data.

The evidence shows two distinct mechanisms operating in different covenantal modes. (1) External-demand mechanism (Sinai/Mosaic covenant): law given externally, demanding internal obedience. The mechanism is instruction + discipline + sanction (oath/curse for breach — OBS-034-007). Change is expected through sustained volitional compliance with the external law. This is the discipline-and-demand mechanism. (2) Internal-inscription mechanism (new covenant): God writes on the heart and mind (Heb 8:10 — OBS-034-059); the Spirit administers the covenant from within (2 Cor 3:6 — OBS-034-061). The mechanism is divine interior action — inscription, not instruction. Change is produced from within through God's own writing, not through the person's compliance with an external demand. This is the constitutive-transformation mechanism. The two mechanisms correspond to the OT vs. NT covenantal modes and are the constitutional-level expression of DIM-34-001's Sinai-to-new-covenant trajectory. The shift in mechanism — from external demand to internal inscription — is the most analytically significant developmental finding of T5.

Yes — the covenant vocabulary operates directly in relation to suffering and affliction in several registers. (1) The covenant curse as suffering: Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036) — the curse poured out upon Israel is covenant-consequence suffering. The suffering of exile, sword, famine, and plague in the prophetic literature is explicitly framed as the activated covenant curse — suffering is the form taken by covenant sanction (H0423 alah — OBS-034-007). (2) Covenant as refuge in suffering: Psa 25:10-14 (Group 765-001/765-005 direction) — the covenant-keeper finds God's steadfast love and faithfulness in distress; covenant is the relational ground that holds when circumstances are afflicting. (3) The Noahic covenant as response to the catastrophe of the flood: Gen 9:15 (OBS-034-038) — God's covenant is made in the aftermath of catastrophic destruction. Covenant is God's answer to suffering at the cosmic scale. (4) The new covenant's enactment through Christ's suffering: Heb 9:15 — "a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant" (OBS-034-058 direction). The new covenant is enacted through the covenant-maker's own suffering and death. Covenant is constituted through suffering, not merely responses to it.

Yes — several sequential relationships are evidenced. (1) Covenant → hesed (love/steadfast love): Deu 7:9 — "keeps covenant and steadfast love." The pairing consistently presents covenant first, hesed second — covenant is the relational structure; hesed is the relational quality that flows within it. Lk 1:72 — "to remember his holy covenant, to show the mercy promised." Covenant precedes and grounds the expression of hesed/mercy. (2) Covenant → knowing: Jer 31:33-34 — law on the heart → "they shall all know me." The covenant inscription precedes and produces the knowing. (3) Moral failure → covenant breach → curse: Dan 9:11 — transgression → breach → curse poured out (OBS-034-036). Failure precedes breach; breach activates curse. (4) Covenant → hope: Eph 2:12 — being outside the covenants of promise → having no hope. Covenant inclusion precedes and grounds hope; covenant exclusion precedes and produces hopelessness. (5) New covenant (reception) → new self (formation): Eph 4:24 context — the new covenant's constitutional transformation grounds the formation of the new self.

The sequences are primarily causal and constitutive, not merely correlational. (1) Covenant → hesed: constitutive — the covenant is the structure within which hesed operates; hesed is not possible in the same sustained, committed form outside a covenantal framework. Covenant constitutes the relational structure that makes hesed a sustainable inner-being quality. (2) Covenant inscription → knowing: causal — God's writing on the heart produces the knowing (Jer 31:34 — "I will write on their hearts... they shall all know me"). The inscription is the causal mechanism; the knowing is the product. (3) Moral failure → covenant breach → curse: causal — the breach activates the curse embedded in the oath structure (OBS-034-007, OBS-034-036). The causal chain is morally necessary, not contingent. (4) Covenant → hope: constitutive — covenant inclusion constitutes the conditions for hope (OBS-034-060); hope is not merely correlated with covenant but grounded in it as its foundation. The key finding: covenant is structurally prior to the characteristics it enables — it is the condition of possibility for love (hesed), hope, knowing God, and the new self. This is the constitutional-priority finding that underpins DIM-34-SD001.

Yes — confirmed from §G.3 (OBS-034-032, OBS-034-033): Covenant shares 11 anchor verses with other registries. Key shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 — shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind). (2) Neh 10:29 — shared with R019 (calling). (3) Eph 4:24 — shared with R076 (holiness). (4) Eph 2:12 — shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority). (5) Rom 1:31 — shared with R111 (mercy). (6) Dan 9:24 — shared with R147 (sin). (7) 2Ki 23:3 — shared with R159 (testimony). (8) 2Sa 23:5 — shared with R177 (worth). (9) Rev 2:17 — shared with R204 (name). (10) Isa 28:15 — shared with R078 (hope). These 11 shared-anchor relationships across 10 distinct registries confirm that covenant's anchor verses are structurally important to a wide range of programme registries — covenant is not isolated but serves as an anchor point for multiple inner-being characteristics.

Three most analytically significant shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind): the new covenant's constitutional claim (law written on mind and heart) is an anchor for both the abomination and mind registries — revealing that the new covenant's inner inscription touches the cognitive inner-being territory that both registries engage. The fact that this verse anchors the abomination registry is unexpected and suggests that the abomination registry's engagement with this verse may be through the contrast between the abomination of old-covenant violation and the new-covenant remedy. Requires Session D investigation (SP-034-001 direction). (2) Eph 2:12 shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority): covenant and hope share this anchor, revealing that covenantal standing is the ground of hope (OBS-034-060) — and that authority-structures also anchor on this verse, suggesting that covenant intersects with authority in the domain of inclusion/exclusion from divine authority-structures. (3) Rom 1:31 shared with R111 (mercy): the covenantal defect (asunthetos) and mercy share an anchor — suggesting that the mercy registry engages the covenant-breaking context of Rom 1:31 as a site where mercy is absent, and that the covenant-mercy relationship requires cross-registry synthesis (SP-034-005).

Yes — the vocabulary makes several such distinctions. (1) Disposition vs. act: berit (noun — the standing bond/disposition) vs. karat berit (verb phrase — the act of cutting/making). The vocabulary distinguishes the resulting reality from the constituting act. (2) Received vs. given: the vocabulary does not have separate terms for receiving vs. giving covenant, but the grammatical structure distinguishes them — God is subject of karat berit in 3304-001 (God gives); the human is subject in 3304-002 and 3304-004 (human gives). Direction is encoded in subject rather than term. (3) Condition vs. quality: the noun berit names the condition (the bond that exists); the karat and shava vocabulary names the quality of the act (the seriousness and binding character of the commitment). The oath vocabulary (H0423 alah, H7650 shava) encodes the moral quality of the covenant-making act — serious, self-binding, potentially self-cursing. (4) Negative form: H3748 keritut (divorce) names the condition of broken covenant; H3772J karat (to lack) names the negative of covenantal continuity. The vocabulary explicitly distinguishes positive and negative covenantal states.

Yes — explicitly. (1) H3748 keritut (divorce — the cutting of the marriage covenant — OBS-034-012): the formal opposite of covenant-union is named by a term from the same cutting root. Divorce is the covenant-opposite explicitly named by covenant's own vocabulary. (2) G0802 asunthetos (untrustworthy/covenant-breaker — OBS-034-018): the NT names the covenant-lacking character type with a specific term — asunthetos = not-placed-together = unable to hold covenants. The structural opposite (covenant-breaking as character defect) has its own lexical item in the NT. (3) H3772J karat (to cut: lack — OBS-034-015): the negative of covenantal continuity — being cut off is the covenant-opposite of being in continuous covenant relationship. (4) H0423 alah (the curse embedded in the oath — OBS-034-007): the sanction for covenant-absence is named within the covenant vocabulary itself. The vocabulary is internally comprehensive — it names both the positive reality and its negation.

The full vocabulary arc from Hebrew to Greek to NT reveals a semantic trajectory of deepening and enrichment. At the Hebrew level (berit, karat, shava, alah, amanah): covenant is the solemn, self-binding relational bond constituted through somatic enactment (cutting, blood) and speech (oath), with embedded sanctions (curse), and rooted in faithful character (amanah-emunah). At the Greek level (diathēkē, asunthetos, kainos): covenant becomes also a testamentary inheritance (diathēkē), the new covenant is qualitatively unprecedented (kainos), and covenant-faithlessness is named as a character defect (asunthetos). The full arc reveals: covenant is simultaneously (1) relational — a bond between persons; (2) bodily — enacted through blood, cutting, circumcision; (3) oral — constituted through sworn speech; (4) legal — carrying sanctions, obligations, and inheritance provisions; (5) moral — requiring and producing faithful character; (6) transformative — producing new inner constitution (new covenant); (7) eschatological — oriented toward eternal inheritance and new creation. No single term carries all seven dimensions; the full vocabulary arc is required to see the complete semantic range.

The primary covenant verse evidence is overwhelmingly covenantal-cultic in its contextual setting — which is to say, the verse evidence is typically embedded within the explicit covenantal life of the worshipping community. The primary settings: (1) Covenantal — the covenant renewals, covenant-making ceremonies, and covenant oracles are the primary covenantal-formal context (Exo 24; Deu 29; Neh 9-10; Jer 31; Heb 8). (2) Judicial — Dan 9:11 (the covenant curse activated by moral failure); Heb 10:29 (the judgment for profaning the covenant blood). The covenant operates within a judicial framework that enforces its sanctions. (3) Liturgical — 1 Cor 11:25; Mat 26:28 (the Eucharistic words of institution). The covenant is enacted and recalled within the worshipping community's ritual. (4) Eschatological — Rev 11:19 (the ark in the heavenly temple); Rev 21:1-5 (new creation). What this combination of settings reveals: covenant is not abstract theology but embedded in the concrete practices of worship, law, and community life.

T6 reveals that kindness sits at the structural centre of a dense network of related characteristics rather than on the edge. Its most consistent co-occurrences (faithfulness, justice, compassion, forgiveness, humility, patience) are not merely adjacent virtues but appear to be partially constituent of kindness itself — the Mic 6:8 triad (justice/kindness/humility) and the Eph 4:32 cluster (kindness/tenderheartedness/forgiveness) function as composite wholes where each element reinforces and partly defines the others. The causal network is explicit and directional: divine kindness → praise + repentance in the recipient → reproduced human kindness toward others → sustained covenant relationships. This causal chain means kindness is not only a characteristic that one possesses but one that is structurally self-propagating through its own operation. The semantic-root finding is analytically significant: the CHESED root family spans the noun (chesed — kindness), the verb (chasad — be kind), the person (chasid — the kind one), the negative (shame/reproach for its failure), and a proper name (Chesed) — a root family that covers definition, action, identity, consequence, and proper-noun instantiation within a single family. T6's most significant intra-tier finding: Mic 6:8 anchors eight registries simultaneously, which confirms that kindness is not a standalone quality but a node in a structural web of C17 cluster characteristics — its full meaning requires these other registries to be seen.

The full vocabulary arc for kindness spans Hebrew and Greek, noun and verb, abstract quality and embodied person, genuine expression and named counterfeit, positive attribute and negative consequence — and this breadth is itself analytically significant. Reading the arc as a whole: (a) The Hebrew root family (H2617A chesed — loyal kindness as noun; H2616A chasad — to be kind as verb; H2623 chasid — the kind person as identity; H2617B — shame/reproach as the negative consequence of its failure) covers the complete semantic circuit: what kindness is, how it is enacted, who embodies it, and what happens when it is absent or fails. The inclusion of shame as the negative of chesed reveals that chesed operates within an honour-covenant framework — its failure brings reproach, not merely disappointment [OBS-099-008]. (b) The Greek root family (G5543 chrēstos — kind as quality; G5544 chrēstotēs — kindness as abstract noun; G5541 chrēsteuomai — to act kindly as verb; G5542 chrēstologia — pleasant speech as counterfeit) similarly covers quality, abstraction, action, and counterfeit. The coinage of chrēstologia as a specific term for the counterfeit reveals that the Greek-speaking community around Paul had encountered false-kindness frequently enough to need a named category for it [OBS-099-021/023]. (c) Taken together, the vocabulary arc reveals that kindness is semantically rich in both Testaments — it is not a thin peripheral term but a conceptually dense one with its own vocabulary of enactment (verb forms), personification (chasid), negative (shame, severity), and falsification (chrēstologia). The complete semantic range encompasses: loyal covenant love (chesed), benign quality of character (chrēstos), the Spirit-produced virtue (chrēstotēs), the active exercise of love in the kind mode (chrēsteuomai), the person whose identity is shaped by this loyalty (chasid), and the imitative counterfeit (chrēstologia). This arc from concept to embodiment to action to falsification reveals kindness as one of the programme's most structurally complete vocabulary fields.

The fulfilment of peace in Christ reveals that the original design was for the human person to live in the peace of God — not a condition achieved by the person but a Person known. The "YHWH-Shalom" altar name (406-001) names God as peace in the context of a theophany — the human person encountering God, receiving peace, and naming God as its source and substance. The fulfilment in Christ (Eph 2:14 — he himself is our peace) reveals that this was always the design: peace is not an abstract inner state but a relational reality grounded in the presence of the one who IS peace. The original design for the human person was to dwell in God's peace through covenant fellowship — and the NT peace in Christ is the restoration and fulfilment of that original design. The "dividing wall of hostility" broken down (Eph 2:14) is not a new problem solved but an original rupture healed.

Peace enables agency — the peaceful person is constitutionally equipped to act in the world from a stable inner base rather than from reactive anxiety. Evidence: (1) Dan 10:19 — "peace be with you; be strong and of good courage" — the angelic peace-gift produces strength and courage for action; peace precedes and enables Daniel's capacity to act on the vision; (2) Isa 32:17 — "the effect of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness, quietness and trust forever" — the peace condition produces trust, which is the basis for courageous action; (3) Deu 28:65 reverse — the absence of peace produces the "trembling heart" and "languishing soul" that impair agency; fearful and anxious people cannot act decisively. Conversely: complacent ease (H7600 group 413-001) impairs agency by producing false security and contempt for consequences — a corrupted form of "peace" that disengages agency.

Yes — the data shows different modes of operation. Within existing covenant relationship: God's covenant of peace (Isa 54:10) is addressed to those already in covenant with him — it deepens and confirms the existing relationship. Within existing friendship: H7965J (2510-001) — the sha.lom-friendship bond is most dramatically revealed when it is violated (Psa 41:9, Jer 20:10) — the betrayal is devastating precisely because the relationship was pre-existing. In new relationships: G1514 eirēneuō (Rom 12:18 — "with all") — the call to peace extends to all people, including strangers; Jer 29:7 — seeking the welfare of the exile city involves extending peace to a new (and hostile) community. Eph 2:14 — Christ making peace between Jew and Gentile is the formation of a new relationship between previously hostile parties. The data does not show a sharp distinction in the characteristic itself — peace is offered in both contexts — but the vulnerability in existing relationships (betrayal wounding) and the cost in new relationships (hostile parties) are different.

Yes — peace produces transformation at multiple levels: (1) Inner condition change: 1Sa 1:17-18 — Hannah departs after the blessing and "her face was no longer sad" — the peace-word transforms her inner emotional state immediately; (2) Orientation change: Isa 26:3 — the mind's orientation toward God produces perfect peace, which then shapes how the person views all of reality; (3) Self-understanding: Isa 38:17 — Hezekiah's retrospective ("it was for my welfare that I had great bitterness") — the receipt of peace through suffering transforms his self-understanding of the suffering; suffering is reread as the path to welfare; (4) Relational transformation: Eph 2:14-16 — Christ's peace breaks down the dividing wall, producing new corporate identity (one body); formerly hostile groups are transformed into one; (5) Character transformation: Gal 5:22 — Spirit-produced peace forms the person's character over time.

All four relationships appear: (1) Produces: Heb 12:11 — discipline produces peaceful fruit in those trained; Isa 38:17 — bitterness was the path to welfare; suffering is the generative mechanism in certain contexts; (2) Tests: Lam 3:17 — the soul bereft of peace is the person whose peace is tested to its limit; the sustained peacelessness under catastrophe tests whether the person's peace has deep roots or is situational; (3) Reveals: the person who maintains peace through suffering reveals a peace grounded in God rather than circumstance (Phili 4:7 — "surpasses all understanding" suggests it transcends circumstantial explanation); (4) Deepens: the retrospective memory of peace-through-suffering (Isa 38:17) deepens understanding of God's ways; suffering that produces peace creates a richer experiential knowledge of shalom than suffering-free peace. The paradox of T1 (Q&A-023) is confirmed here: suffering is not peace's enemy but its formative companion in the data.

Two verses serve as joint primary anchors across the corpus — one OT, one NT: (1) OT primary anchor: Isa 26:3 — "You keep him in perfect peace [shalom shalom] whose mind is stayed on you, because he trusts in you." This verse names: the divine agent (God as keeper), the condition (shalom shalom — the doubled superlative), the constitutional location (mind — the directed mind), and the ground (trust). It is the most complete single-verse statement of the inner-peace reality. (2) NT primary anchor: Eph 2:14 — "For he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility." This verse names: the person of peace (Christ = peace — ontological identification), the accomplishment (made one), the mechanism (his flesh/cross), and the scope (between peoples). Together these two verses — one naming the experience (shalom shalom for the trust-directed mind, Isa 26:3) and one naming the person (Christ IS peace, Eph 2:14) — constitute the definitive biblical statement of what peace is.

T5 establishes peace's unique dual role in the sanctification arc: it is simultaneously the condition under which sanctification proceeds (1Th 5:23 — the God of peace sanctifies) and the fruit that sanctification produces (Gal 5:22 — Spirit-fruit; Heb 12:11 — peaceful fruit of righteousness through discipline). This dual role means: (1) The person who persistently refuses peace is simultaneously refusing the conditions for their own sanctification; (2) The progress of sanctification is measurable partly by the deepening and stabilising of inner peace. The suffering connection (T5.4) adds: peace is not only formed in comfort but produced through suffering (Isa 53:5, Heb 12:11, Isa 38:17) — suffering is peace's formative companion, not its enemy.

The data provides partial evidence. For covenantal giving (872-001): the condition evidenced is being in a covenant relationship with God — the giving of land is addressed to Abraham and his offspring, not to all humanity indiscriminately. The covenant relationship is the precondition of reception. For compassionate giving (872-002): the condition evidenced is the presenting of genuine inner need — Leah is hated and suffering; God hears and gives (OBS-060-037). Expressed need or suffering appears to enable responsive giving. For eschatological giving (872-007): no precondition is named in the anchor verses — those who mourn receive beauty (Isa 61:3), suggesting mourning itself is the enabling condition. For human self-giving (Job 1:21, Isa 50:6): the enabling inner condition is trust/covenantal identity — Job's worship proceeds from recognising divine sovereignty; the Servant gives himself because he trusts divine vindication. Gap: the data does not address whether ongoing faithfulness or human moral condition affects the receptivity to divine covenantal giving — the texts name the giving as God's initiative, not as contingent on human merit.

The hearing faculty is explicitly engaged in two instances. Gen 29:33 (OBS-060-037): "the Lord has heard (shama) that I am hated" — divine hearing is the perceptive act that precedes and grounds the compassionate giving. The perception of inner suffering is the precondition of the responsive giving. Neh 9:20 (OBS-060-040): God gives Spirit "to instruct them" — the Spirit-giving produces the capacity to hear/receive instruction, engaging the perceptive-receptive dimension at the cognitive-formative level. For the human direction: Jer 13:16 (OBS-060-047) — "before your feet stumble... while you look for light" — visual perception (looking for light) is present in the failure-to-give-glory context, though as the condition of the person who has not given glory rather than as a faculty engaged by giving itself. Hearing is the primary perceptive faculty engaged.

The Spirit-giving (Neh 9:20) and prophetic warning (Neh 9:30) in the endowing and covenantal modes can be read as conscience-forming and conscience-engaging: God's Spirit and prophets address the inner moral witness. The refusal to give ear to prophetic warning represents a suppression of conscience-input that leads to judicial giving-over. In this sense the characteristic (Spirit-giving, warning-giving) enables conscience, and its rejection impairs it. The eschatological giving that produces "oaks of righteousness" (Isa 61:3) would imply a restored moral inner life including conscience. Gap: these are inferences from the data, not direct statements. The data is richer on moral evaluation (T3.8) than on conscience specifically.

Yes — relational capacity is intrinsic to the characteristic. Every classified use of na.tan in the inner-being register involves a giver and a receiver in genuine relational engagement. Group 872-002 most directly engages relational capacity: God gives in direct response to Leah's inner condition of being hated (Gen 29:33, OBS-060-037) — this presupposes divine attentiveness to the relational situation of the other. Hos 11:8 (OBS-060-038) shows the relational depth of divine giving: the heart recoils, compassion grows warm — these are movements of genuine relational connection, not administrative decision. For the human: Isa 50:6 — the Servant's self-giving is an act of relational fidelity. Job 1:21 — the relinquishing blessing is addressed to the Lord (not internal) — it is a relational act toward God. Group 872-006 (glory-giving) is structurally about the proper orientation of the divine-human relational connection.

Enabling conditions for reception (drawn from Q&A-031 and the anchor verse pattern): (1) Covenant participation: being within the relational structure that God's giving constitutes. The land-giving texts presuppose covenant membership as the reception condition. (2) Presented inner need: compassionate giving is triggered by visible inner suffering (Leah's condition, Gen 29:33) — the authentic expression of need enables responsive reception. (3) Posture of mourning/depletion: the eschatological giving is addressed to "those who mourn in Zion" (Isa 61:3) — the state of acknowledged need and depleted inner life. Blocking conditions: (1) Idolatrous misattribution — receiving the gift but attributing it to Baal (Hos 2:8, Q&A-032) blocks genuine reception even when the material gift arrives. (2) Covenant violation — faithlessness inverts giving-to into giving-over (Q&A-032), removing the receptive condition. (3) Refusal to give ear — Neh 9:30 — the rejection of prophetic warning closes the channel of Spirit-mediated reception.

Yes — suffering is a pervasive context for the characteristic across three groups. (1) As the trigger for compassionate giving (872-002): Leah's suffering (being hated) triggers divine giving (Gen 29:33, OBS-060-037). Hosea's Israel suffering from lack, drought, and need is the context of giving in the broader 872-002 corpus. (2) As the context within which human self-giving is enacted (872-005): the Servant gives his back to those who strike — bodily suffering is the medium of the self-giving (Isa 50:6, OBS-060-045). Job's devastating losses are the context of his blessing-response (Job 1:21, OBS-060-044). (3) As the condition that eschatological giving addresses (872-007): "those who mourn in Zion" — mourning/grief is the before-state that the eschatological giving transforms (Isa 61:3, OBS-060-048). The characteristic operates as: response to suffering (compassionate mode), act within suffering (self-giving mode), and remedy for suffering (eschatological mode).

findings_paired_keyword.text: Contempt vocabulary in Registry 190 operates at three levels: (a) the inner disposition of contempt as evaluative stance (Character/Disposition), (b) contempt as social-relational dynamic (Relational/Social), (c) contempt with theological weight directed at God, his word, or his servant (Moral/Conscience, Theological/Divine-Human). Session B should map how these three registers of contempt relate in the inner being.

The new covenant transformation group (3272-001: diatithēmi — the covenantal act writing divine law on the inner mind and heart) appears in the abomination registry. This places the new covenant's inner transformation as the divine answer to abomination and inner defilement. Session B should examine whether the abomination cluster's verse evidence shows the new covenant specifically as the remedy for abomination — and whether this pattern recurs across the cluster.

The characteristic is called 'love' — a name that in English collapses a vocabulary of multiple Hebrew and Greek terms into a single referent. The name signals an orientation outward: love is always toward something or someone; it cannot be self-contained. Even the word's grammar in Scripture consistently requires a direct object (love of God, love of neighbour, love of enemy, love of money). The name also signals relational constitution: the one who loves is partly constituted by who and what they love. The programme's description note ('most foundational and most theologically dense word in the entire corpus,' OBS-103-004) signals that the name functions as an organising category for the entire inner-being vocabulary.

Working description: Love is the primary inner-being orientation of the human person — the directed attachment of the whole inner self toward God, persons, and the good, operating as both the deepest constitutional disposition and the foundational act of the will, producing and sustaining all relational bonds. It locates principally at the heart-soul interface (Spirit-Soul classification confirmed). It engages the will (commanded), the cognition (requires knowledge of the beloved), the emotion (produces joy, grief, rest depending on the beloved's presence/absence), and the body (expressed somatically in kiss, embrace, tears). Its impact: it constitutes the person's relational identity (who you love defines who you are), organises all other inner-being characteristics (love of God requires hatred of evil; love of God produces fruit), and is the externally legible mark of the community's identity (Joh 13:35). Its absence is the diagnostic indicator of the divine image's distortion (1Jo 4:8).

The structural opposite is hatred (sane — H8130, 139 active verses, OWNER term, OBS-103-030). Sane is embedded in R103 as an OWNER term — the programme treats hate as constitutive of the love vocabulary's boundary, not external to it. Lev 19:17 ('you shall not hate your brother in your heart') places hate as the inner alternative to love of neighbour (OBS-103-114). Pro 10:12 ('hatred stirs up strife, love covers all offences,' OBS-103-097) places them as causal-opposite forces with opposed social effects. The secondary structural opposite at the ultimate level is enmity with God: friendship with the world is enmity (echthra) with God (Jam 4:4, OBS-103-058). But hatred is the primary inner-being opposite — it is the same inner mechanism as love (directed orientation) running in the opposed direction.

1Cor 13:4-7 provides the programme's most explicit catalogue of what love excludes: envy, boasting, arrogance, rudeness, self-seeking, irritability, resentfulness, rejoicing in wrongdoing (OBS-103-127). The list names love's resistances across moral, relational, and emotional registers. At the structural level, love resists division of ultimate allegiance (Jam 4:4 — cannot love God and world simultaneously, OBS-103-058), resists self-assertion as the primary orientation (love-of-self as the first item in the last-days catalogue, OBS-103-137), and resists the withholding of self (Joh 15:13 — love's maximum is the giving of the soul/life, OBS-103-125). Love also explicitly resists concealment of inner truth from the beloved: Lev 19:17 contrasts hate-as-silence with love-as-frank-correction (OBS-103-114).

What love is not, from the data: love is not sentiment — it is commanded (Mat 22:37) and exercised irrespective of feeling (enemy-love, Mat 5:44). Love is not mere natural affection — phileō is insufficient as the full standard; agapē and chesed exceed phileō in obligatory reach. Love is not the same as desire (R043) — the desire registry owns the primary ahav verbal form (H0157G); desire and love share a mechanism but the programme treats them as distinct (OBS-103-010, OBS-103-022). Love is not the same as goodness (R067) — the tov family is XREF; love produces goodness but is not identical with it (OBS-103-014). The precise boundary between love and compassion (R023), kindness (R099), and mercy (R111) cannot be fully drawn from Stage 2a evidence alone — these are the programme's adjacent characteristics whose distinctions require dedicated treatment in Session D. Partial: the internal boundary is evidenced; the inter-registry distinctions are named as gaps deferred to Session D.

Four primary modes of operation, each evidenced across the anchor verse corpus:
(1) **Covenantal-dispositional mode** (chesed, agapē): Love as stable inner orientation of faithful loyalty — not feeling-contingent, not revocable by the beloved's failure. God's chesed 'never ceases' (Lam 3:22, OBS-103-088); human chesed is written on the heart as character (Pro 3:3, OBS-103-090). This mode operates at the level of character — the deep stable orientation.
(2) **Compassionate-responsive mode** (racham, splanchnizō): Love that is activated by the beloved's suffering — the inner-organ movement (OBS-103-105, OBS-103-116). This mode responds to need; it is not passive but activated.
(3) **Volitional-costly mode** (agapē — enemy-love, self-giving): Love that operates against the inner grain of natural preference. Mat 5:44 (OBS-103-143), Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125). This mode requires the will's deliberate engagement to exceed what natural affection would produce.
(4) **Affective-relational mode** (phileō, ahavah-intense, dod): Love as naturally felt warm attachment — the affection for friends, the longing for the beloved. This mode is experienced; it is love in its felt dimension.
The four modes are not always distinguishable in a single verse but are analytically distinct operations of the same inner orientation.

Yes — context and direction both shape the mode. Direction: love toward God operates in the volitional-dispositional mode most prominently (commanded; requires the whole person — Mat 22:37, OBS-103-139); love toward the neighbour operates in both the dispositional and the costly-volitional modes; love toward the beloved (marital/erotic) operates in the affective-intense mode (dod, ahavah — Song vocabulary, OBS-103-041); love toward the enemy operates exclusively in the costly-volitional mode (OBS-103-143). Constitutional level: at the spirit/soul boundary (sb_classification confirmed), love appears to have its deepest location at the spirit level (the willed, faithful chesed) and its experiential expression at the soul level (the felt ahavah/phileō). At the heart level (OBS-103-126) love is infused by the Spirit and inscribed as character. The constitutional level shapes how love presents: deep-spirit love is more chesed-like; soul-surface love is more phileō-like.

Yes — evidenced across several groups. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): love's communicative mode is knowledge-disclosure — Jesus names the content of his friendship with the disciples as 'all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.' Love shares the inner world of the lover with the beloved. Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): frank correction (hocheach tochi'ach — honest rebuke) is love's communicative act, in contrast to hate's silence. The kiss (filēma, katafileō — OBS-103-054, OBS-103-056) is love's somatic-communicative mode — the body's expression of the inner word. The divine declaration ('this is my beloved Son' — Mat 3:17, OBS-103-120) is love's constitutive speech act: the naming of belovedness is itself the conferral of identity. Love therefore has a speech-mode that functions as: disclosure (sharing the inner world), correction (honest engagement), and declaration (constitutive naming).

The response varies by the type of love-encounter. When divine love is received: the immediate response is love-in-return (1Jo 4:19) or rest (Zep 3:17, Psa 127:2). When love is expressed at cost (forgiveness-love, enemy-love): the immediate response in the giver is not named directly. When love is lost: the immediate response is grief (Song 5:6, OBS-103-062; 2Sa 1:26, OBS-103-095). When the beloved is encountered: the immediate response is delight and longing (Song 2:16, OBS-103-061; Luk 15:20 — the father's compassion-movement at sight of the returning son, OBS-103-057). The variation is systematic, not random: the type of encounter determines the response type. There is no single invariant immediate response to love — the response depends on the direction of the encounter (giving vs receiving vs losing vs encountering).

The data provides several instances: (a) Joh 3:19 (OBS-103-147) — the person who encounters light-as-love and does not receive it 'loved the darkness' — the inner state is commitment to the alternative; the non-reception is an inner-being act of love misdirected. (b) Luk 16:14 (OBS-103-136) — the Pharisees who were money-lovers ridiculed Jesus' teaching; their inner state is intellectual rejection grounded in love-of-money. (c) 2Ti 4:10 (OBS-103-148) — Demas, in love with the present world, deserts: the non-reception of love-community is an act of love toward the world instead. The pattern is consistent: the person who does not receive love is not loveless — they are loving the wrong thing. Lovelessness in the sense of having no love-capacity does not appear in the data; what appears is love misdirected.

Secondary dimensions evidenced: (a) **Moral Character** — chesed written on the heart (Pro 3:3, OBS-103-090), chasid as the person of covenant-character (OBS-103-026), love-of-good (filagathos) as a character disposition (OBS-103-134). Love as stable character (not just act) carries Moral Character alongside Relational Disposition. (b) **Emotion-Positive** — the affective-relational modes (phileō, ahavah-intense, dod) carry positive emotional content: delight, joy, longing (OBS-103-061, OBS-103-112). (c) **Volition** — enemy-love (Mat 5:44) and the ranking of loves (Mat 10:37) are explicitly volitional — choices against natural preference. These secondary dimensions do not compete with Relational Disposition; they describe the faculties through which love's relational orientation is expressed. The primary dimension correctly captures the constitutive structure; the secondary dimensions capture the modes.

Yes — emphatically. The commanded form of love ('you shall love' — Mat 22:37, Lev 19:18) requires the will's exercise — love cannot be merely a spontaneous feeling if it is commanded. Enemy-love (Mat 5:44, OBS-103-143) is the clearest evidence: loving enemies requires volitional engagement against natural preference. The ranking of loves (Mat 10:37, OBS-103-151) requires a deliberate choice-act — placing love of Christ above natural love of family. Ruth's chesed (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) — choosing the elderly Boaz over young men — is love expressed through will against self-interest. The will's engagement is not peripheral but central to love in its highest form (agapē, chesed). The affective forms (phileō, dod) may be less volitionally constituted, but the command-forms require the will.

Both operate in the data — love both requires and produces will-acts. It requires the will: enemy-love (Mat 5:44), ranking of loves (Mat 10:37), and Ruth's choice (Rut 3:10) are all volitional love-acts. It produces will-acts: love of Christ produces commandment-keeping (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140) — the inner orientation generates the volitional pattern of life. The two modes are distinct: love-as-act-of-will (requiring deliberate engagement) vs love-as-generator-of-will (the inner orientation that produces volitional patterns). Both are evidenced. The affective forms (phileō, dod) may operate without explicit volitional initiation, but they too produce will-acts (Peter's fileō generates faithful mission-response, Joh 21:17, OBS-103-153).

Indirect evidence only. The conscience (suneidēsis) does not appear by name in R103's anchor verses. However: Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114) — the prohibition on hating the brother 'in your heart' and the command to rebuke frankly — implies conscience-engagement: the inner recognition that hate is wrong and that honest love requires action. 2Cor 11:26 (OBS-103-067) and Gal 2:4 (OBS-103-068) — the false brother's concealed inner orientation — implies conscience is involved in the recognition of inner deception. The programme's suneidēsis registry (R026) co-occurs with love at Rom 8:29 (shared anchor). Gap: conscience's specific engagement with love is not explicitly evidenced in R103's anchor corpus. The conscience-love connection is inferred from the moral imperative structure of the love commands.

Love is the programme's most fully integrated constitutional characteristic. The integration: at the spirit/will level — love is commanded (Mat 22:37), covenantal (chesed), and willed (enemy-love, Mat 5:44); at the soul level — love is experienced (Song 5:6 — soul failing at beloved's absence), given (Joh 15:13 — soul laid down for friends), and constitutive of identity (nephesh of God has a beloved, Jer 12:7); at the heart level — love is inscribed (Pro 3:3), infused by the Spirit (Rom 5:5), and the seat of love-awareness (Song 5:2); at the mind level — love requires and produces knowledge (Joh 15:15, Hos 6:6), and directs cognitive attention to the lovely (Phili 4:8); at the body level — love is expressed through kiss, embrace, tears, the movement of inner organs (Unit 7 multiple instances). No constitutional level is absent from the love vocabulary. Love is the programme's most constitutionally comprehensive characteristic — it operates at all levels simultaneously.

Will-engagement reveals that love in the biblical data is not reducible to feeling — it is a chosen orientation that the person actively maintains and directs. Enemy-love (Mat 5:44, OBS-103-143) is the most demanding evidence: love directed against natural antipathy requires the will's deliberate and sustained engagement. This reveals that love has depth precisely at the point where feeling runs out — chesed persists when feeling does not (Lam 3:22, OBS-103-088). The will is love's constitutional ground when affective experience is withdrawn or absent. This is the programme's key distinguishing feature for love versus mere positive feeling: love can sustain itself volitionally when the soul-level affective experience is absent.

The indirect evidence suggests conscience and love operate at the same moral depth but through different mechanisms. The conscience recognises the moral wrongness of hate-in-the-heart (Lev 19:17, OBS-103-114); love is what the conscience directs the person toward as the alternative. If conscience is the inner moral monitor, love is the inner moral orientation — the conscience identifies what violates love; love provides the positive orientation the conscience is protecting. Gap: this relationship is inferred, not directly evidenced in R103's anchor verses.

Yes — through the judgment of the love-object's worth and the assessment of love's hierarchy. Mat 10:37 (OBS-103-151) requires a judgment: 'whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me' — love operates through a comparative judgment of worth among competing objects. The ranking of loves (OBS-103-022 — love as four-mode structure) requires reason to navigate. Jam 4:4 (OBS-103-058): the reasoning that friendship with the world constitutes enmity with God is a logical judgment. Pro 10:12 (OBS-103-097): 'love covers all offences' — the judgment that covering is better than exposing is a reasoned moral assessment. Love requires and shapes the judgment-faculty at the level of determining what and whom to love, in what order, and how.

Yes — love is the primary shaper of moral sensitivity in the data. Psa 97:10 (OBS-103-115): 'O you who love the Lord, hate evil' — love of God directly produces moral sensitivity to evil (the capacity to hate what is wrong). Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): the prohibition on hating the brother in the heart implies that moral sensitivity recognises inner hate as morally significant. 1Cor 13:6 (within OBS-103-127): love 'does not rejoice in wrongdoing' — love shapes the moral sensitivity that recognises wrongdoing as something not to be celebrated. Pro 10:12 (OBS-103-097): love's moral sensitivity distinguishes between hatred-that-stirs-strife and love-that-covers — the faculty that makes this distinction is morally sensitised by love. Love functions as the moral-sensitivity calibrator: it determines what the inner person registers as morally significant and how they register it.

The conscience-as-faculty relationship to love is addressed at T2.7 (Q&A-053, Q&A-062, Q&A-063). The additional faculty-dimension: the conscience functions as the internal monitor that activates when love's requirements are violated (hating the brother in the heart, Lev 19:17) or when love's form is distorted (false brotherhood, Gal 2:4). Love provides the positive standard; conscience provides the monitoring mechanism. The faculty-relationship: love is the norm; conscience is the norm-enforcer in the inner person. Gap: the direct conscience-love faculty interaction is not named in R103's anchor verses. The relationship is structurally inferred.

Love as given outward is the primary ethical direction of the commandment structure: love your neighbour (Lev 19:18), love your enemy (Mat 5:44), love one another (Joh 13:34). The giving of love reaches its maximum in Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125) — 'greater love has no one than this: that someone lay down his life/soul for his friends.' The gift of the soul is love's outer limit. The giving-forms span: chesed as daily covenantal faithfulness given to the covenant-partner (OBS-103-089); agapē given to the enemy against natural preference (OBS-103-143); phileō given as warmth within the community (OBS-103-119); rachamim given as compassion toward the suffering (OBS-103-105). Love-as-given is the primary ethical form in which the inner orientation is concretely actualised.

Human-to-human love is the ethical core of the love commandment. The data shows it operating in five structural relationships: (a) **Neighbour** (H7453 rea — 173 active) — the primary OT ethical form: 'love your neighbour as yourself' (Lev 19:18); (b) **Enemy** (Mat 5:44, OBS-103-143) — the most demanding NT extension — love directed to those who actively oppose the person; (c) **Community of faith** (Joh 13:34, 1Th 3:12) — mutual love as the community's defining practice; (d) **Friendship** (filos, H0157H, dod vocabulary) — personal loyal love; (e) **Family** (filadelfia, filostorgos, filoteknos, filandros) — love within natural and spiritual family structures. The human-to-human direction is the most evidentially diverse — spanning obligation (neighbour-love), choice (friendship), natural bond (family), and cost (enemy-love).

Breakdowns evidenced: (a) **False brotherhood** — Gal 2:4 (OBS-103-068): using the community-love structure to serve a contrary agenda — the most dangerous breakdown because it uses love's form; (b) **Betrayal-kiss** — Luk 22:48 (OBS-103-054): love's somatic sign (kiss) used to indicate enmity — betrayal as the weaponisation of love's language; (c) **Desertion for world-love** — 2Ti 4:10 (OBS-103-148): abandonment of love-community for love of world; (d) **Poverty-caused dissolution** — Pro 19:7 (OBS-103-073): friendship tested by adversity may fail when it was conditional; (e) **Pride-driven rivalry** — Luk 22:24 (OBS-103-131): love-of-primacy at the table of the Lord. Conditions for maintenance: honest engagement (Lev 19:17, frank rebuke), soul-purification (1Pe 1:22), shared suffering (1Pe 5:9). The most dangerous breakdown is false brotherhood — it operates inside the love-structure while working against it.

Extensively — as noted in the XREF analysis (Unit 2, OBS-103-007 through OBS-103-015): (a) **Compassion (R023)**: racham/rachamim family shared — the compassion vocabulary is OWNER in R103 and primary in R023; (b) **Goodness (R067)**: tov/agathos family — H2896A-C tov is XREF in R103, OWNER in R067; (c) **Desire (R043)**: ahav verbal root (H0157G) is XREF in R103, primary in R043; (d) **Peace (R117)**: eirēnē family is XREF in R103; (e) **Hatred**: sane is OWNER in R103, also relevant to other registries. The term sharing ratio (0.701 — 96 of 137 terms shared, OBS-103-006) is among the highest in the programme. Love shares vocabulary with almost every adjacent registry in C17.

Agapē's semantic range in the NT evidence: (a) divine love for humanity (Joh 3:16); (b) human love for God (Mat 22:37); (c) mutual love in community (Joh 13:34); (d) love of enemy (Mat 5:44); (e) disordered love (love of darkness — Joh 3:19; love of the world — 2Ti 4:10; love of the pre-eminent position — 3Jo 9). The semantic range spans divine, human-covenantal, communal, and misdirected forms. Chesed's range: divine faithfulness, human loyalty, the person-type (chasid), the failure of chesed (withdrawal, demand). The combined semantic range of the primary terms covers the full moral landscape — from the highest (God's love as his being) to the lowest (love misdirected toward darkness). This breadth is unique in the programme — no other characteristic has primary terms whose semantic range spans from the ontologically highest (divine nature) to the morally inverted (love of darkness).

Yes — embedded as OWNER. H8130 sane (to hate, 139 active / 156 total) is the structural opposite within the registry (OBS-103-030). H8131 sena (hating, 1 active) is a second form. The hate vocabulary is not XREF but OWNER — the programme treats the structural opposite as constituent of the love vocabulary's boundary (OBS-103-019). Additionally: the philo-compound negative forms (afilarguros — not-money-loving; pseudadelfos — false-brother) name the absence of specific love-forms. The disordered love vocabulary names love's distorted presence rather than its absence. True lovelessness (absence without misdirection) is not prominently named — the data suggests the inner mechanism is always present and always directed at something; the question is always the object, not the presence or absence of love-capacity.

The full vocabulary arc reveals love as the programme's most semantically comprehensive characteristic. The arc spans: (a) **Ground of being** — 1Jo 4:8 (God is love — the ontological summit); (b) **Covenantal permanence** — chesed (never ceasing, Lam 3:22); (c) **Somatic compassion** — rachamim (womb-like movement, Isa 49:15); (d) **Erotic-intense** — dod, ahavah (Song vocabulary, OBS-103-041); (e) **Natural affection** — phileō (Joh 11:36); (f) **Volitional self-giving** — agapē (enemy-love, soul-laying-down); (g) **Object-directed attachment** — philo-compounds (love of money, God, pleasure, brothers); (h) **Structural opposite** — sane (hatred). The arc is a spectrum from the ontologically highest (divine being) through the morally normative (covenantal chesed, commanded agapē) to the naturally affective (phileō, dod) to the morally disordered (love of darkness, self, money). No characteristic in the programme covers a comparable arc. Love is the vocabulary's outermost circle — encompassing the greatest range of inner-being phenomena under a single governing concept.

Yes — and the data makes this explicit. Psa 97:10 (OBS-103-115): 'O you who love the Lord, hate evil' — hatred of evil is commanded of those who love God. Righteous hatred is not love's failure but love's necessary complement: you cannot love God and be indifferent to what opposes him. Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): 'you shall not hate your brother in your heart' — hatred of the brother is wrong. The same inner-being mechanism (hatred as directed-aversion) can be rightly directed (at evil) or wrongly directed (at the brother). This is the precise structural parallel to love: love rightly directed (toward God and neighbour) vs love wrongly directed (toward money, pleasure, world). Hatred operates under the same moral logic as love: the mechanism is morally neutral; the object determines the moral quality. The ordered inner life requires both rightly directed love AND rightly directed hatred.

Yes — and the data names the direction explicitly. The reorganisation operates in three directions: (a) **Restructuring of kinship** — Mat 12:50 (OBS-103-063): the family is restructured around shared orientation toward God rather than natural kinship. Love's community replaces the natural family as the primary social unit. (b) **Inversion of competitive hierarchy** — Rom 12:10 (OBS-103-119): 'outdo one another in showing honor' — the social competition is reversed from honour-claiming to honour-giving. Love reorganises from rivalry to generosity. (c) **Inclusion of the excluded** — Mat 5:44 (OBS-103-143): enemy-love includes those whom natural social dynamics exclude. The direction of love's social reorganisation is consistently: from exclusion toward inclusion, from taking toward giving, from natural kinship toward chosen community. The community constituted by love (Joh 13:35) is a social body organised on principles that invert the natural social order.

Love's definition resolves to a structured family of inner-being orientations sharing one mechanism — directed attachment toward an object — whose moral character is entirely determined by the object chosen. Within this shared mechanism, the vocabulary distinguishes three structural registers: love as disposition (the stable inner orientation — chesed, agapē as character), love as act (the commanded and exercised expression — agapaō, enemy-love), and love as condition (the identity-constituting state of being-loved — agapētos). These are not three separate loves but three aspects of love's single operation: the person has a deep stable orientation (disposition), exercises it in specific acts (act), and exists within the love-relationship as constituted by it (condition). The vocabulary arc runs from the ontologically highest (God is love — 1Jo 4:8) through the morally normative (chesed, commanded agapē) to the affective-natural (phileō, dod) to the morally inverted (love of darkness, love of self). No other characteristic in the programme commands a comparable vocabulary range. The definition's boundary with adjacent characteristics (compassion, mercy, kindness) is partially drawn: love is the broader orientation; compassion is love's suffering-triggered mode; mercy and kindness are love's formal relational expressions. The boundaries require Session D precision.

Love engages all eleven inner faculties, but five show the richest engagement: (1) Identity and self-understanding — love is the primary faculty through which the person knows who they are: being-loved (agapētos) constitutes identity from the outside; what-one-loves constitutes identity from the inside. The person is doubly defined by love. (2) Relational capacity — love is the faculty's active content: it builds (Joh 13:34), sustains (Lam 3:22), and restores (Luk 15:20) relational capacity. (3) Memory — love's sustaining faculty: love without memory cannot maintain itself through absence (Jer 31:20 — 'I do remember him still'). (4) Moral sensitivity — love calibrates what the inner person registers as morally significant; the love-of-God requirement generates the hate-evil requirement (Psa 97:10). (5) Attention — love determines what the person perceives as worth attending to (Phili 4:8 — think on what is lovely). The weaker engagements: creativity (indirect, evidenced through Song-form) and conscience (inferred, not directly named). Overall: love is the faculty-most-engaged characteristic in the programme, operating through perception, reason, memory, imagination, desire, emotion, moral sensitivity, conscience (indirectly), identity, and relational capacity simultaneously.

Love's relational architecture is structured by three directional axes — GOD → HUMAN, HUMAN → GOD, HUMAN → HUMAN — with divine love structurally prior and causally generative of all subsequent love. 1Jo 4:19 ('we love because he first loved us') is the axis-precedence statement: the first axis is not responsive to but constitutive of the second and third. The GOD → HUMAN direction moves through four mechanisms: covenantal declaration (naming the beloved — Jer 12:7, Mat 3:17), Spirit-infusion (Rom 5:5), redemptive acts (Joh 3:16), and covenantal covering (Eze 16:8). The HUMAN → GOD direction requires total inner mobilisation (Mat 22:37 — all heart, soul, mind) and the clearing of rival loves as its condition (1Jo 4:19, 1Ti 6:10). The HUMAN → HUMAN direction spans five structural types (neighbour, enemy, community, friendship, family) and creates the social architecture described in Q&A-088 (restructured kinship, public legibility, competitive generosity). The most significant finding in T4: the human-to-God direction is conditioned by prior reception of divine love — the capacity for human love toward God is itself a gift. The one structural gap: T4.6 (spiritual beings) is absent from the evidence.

Love's definition as directed-attachment-toward-an-object generates a characteristic pattern of faculty engagement. Because love is always toward an object, it necessarily engages attention (what is attended to is the beloved — Phili 4:8), reason (judgment of the object's worth and the hierarchy of competing loves — Mat 10:37), memory (remembering the beloved sustains love — Jer 31:20), and identity (what is loved constitutes who one is — Q&A-075). The definition's object-determination principle (moral quality determined by object) maps onto the moral sensitivity faculty: the faculty that calibrates moral perception is calibrated by love's direction — you notice what you love, and what you love as most important determines what you register as morally significant (Psa 97:10 — love-of-God generates hate-of-evil). The definition's tripartite structure (disposition/act/condition) maps onto the faculty architecture: disposition engages the will and conscience; act engages the relational capacity and body; condition engages identity and emotion. No faculty operates independently of love's fundamental structure.

Love's definition as directed attachment reaches its relational specification in T4's three-axis architecture. The object-determination principle (Q&A-144) — that love's moral quality depends entirely on its object — is enacted relationally when the proper objects are named: God (vertical-ascending), neighbour and enemy (horizontal), and the beloved community (horizontal-mutual). The definition's three registers map onto the relational axes: the dispositional register (stable inner orientation) grounds the covenantal-faithful love in the GOD→HUMAN axis (God's chesed never ceasing — Lam 3:22); the active register (commanded, exercised) grounds the love of neighbour and enemy (Lev 19:18, Mat 5:44); the condition register (being-loved as identity) is the GOD→HUMAN axis's effect in the person (agapētos — constituted as beloved). The relational interfaces reveal what the definition describes abstractly: love's mechanism in concrete directional operation. The most significant inter-tier finding: the definition's requirement that love be directed toward an object is fulfilled eschatologically when the object is perfectly and permanently God — Jer 31:34, 1Jo 4:18 (perfect love).

Love's definition as disposition + act + condition maps onto the developmental sequence in T5. Condition (being-loved) comes first in the sequence — 1Jo 4:19 ('he first loved us') — the person is constituted as beloved before they can love in return. Disposition (stable inner orientation) is formed through the Spirit's infusion (Rom 5:5), obedience-pathway (1Pe 1:22), and training (Tit 2:4) — the formation process produces the stable dispositional form. Act (exercised love) is the formation process's outcome — the person who has been loved, whose disposition has been formed, exercises love toward God, neighbour, and enemy. The reversibility asymmetry (divine love irreversible; human love subject to redirection — Q&A-094) maps onto the definition's three registers: the condition of being-divinely-loved is irreversible (God does not withdraw the beloved-status once conferred — Jer 31:3); the human disposition is subject to redirection through competing loves; the human love-acts can fail without invalidating the conditional ground. The eschatological trajectory (toward perfect love — 1Jo 4:18) describes the movement toward the definition's fullest form: stable disposition + unconstrained act + permanent condition of perfect love.

The constitutional location data and the relational interface data converge on the same structural claim: love's inner depth is its relational reach. The deeper love is constitutionally (heart-inscribed, soul-constitutive, Spirit-sourced), the further its relational reach — from neighbour-love (soul-level reciprocal) to enemy-love (will-level against grain) to divine love (Spirit-level transcendent). The constitutional movement pattern (Spirit → heart → soul → body — Q&A-056) maps onto the relational directional pattern: divine love enters at the deepest constitutional level (Spirit, heart) and produces relational acts at the outermost level (community legibility, bodily expression). The GOD→HUMAN axis operates at the constitutional depth where love is received (heart-infusion — Rom 5:5); the HUMAN→GOD axis requires total constitutional mobilisation (Mat 22:37 — all heart, soul, mind); the HUMAN→HUMAN axis is the outward bodily expression of the internal constitutional reality. Constitutional and relational are thus not two separate dimensions of love but the inside and outside of the same movement.

The vocabulary evidence grounds the faculty claims with lexical precision. Identity faculty (T3.10): agapētos (person-type of being-loved), chasid (person-type of chesed), filos (person-type of phileō) — all three are lexically grounded person-type terms (Q&A-127). Moral sensitivity faculty (T3.8): the hate-vocabulary embedded as OWNER (H8130 sane — Q&A-126) grounds the claim that love requires and produces righteous hatred — the structural opposite provides the moral sensitivity calibration. Memory faculty (T3.3): the verb-form analysis (Q&A-123) shows that chesed as noun-primary form implies a dispositional quality that persists — memory is the chesed-noun's faculty correlate. Attention faculty (T3.1): phileō's etymology (from philos — friend) implies a relational salience structure — the one who is phileō-loved becomes salient to the lover's attention-field. The vocabulary's etymological structure (Q&A-122) is thus the lexical ground of the faculty engagement pattern: the root meanings point toward the faculties each term primarily activates.

The evidential foundation directly supports each of the three relational axes. GOD→HUMAN axis — most densely evidenced: Exo 34:6 (divine self-declaration of love as primary attribute), Jer 12:7 (beloved of God's soul), Mat 3:17 (beloved Son named), Joh 3:16 (God so loved the world), Rom 5:5 (love poured into hearts through Spirit). The lexical evidence for this axis runs from the OT's chesed-God group (169 active verses — largest single group in the registry, Q&A-038) through the NT's agapē-God group (35 relevant). HUMAN→GOD axis — Psa 63:3 (chesed above life), Mat 22:37 (total inner mobilisation), Joh 14:15 (love producing obedience); these are carried by the agapaō-love-of-God group (571-001) and the chesed-human group (536-002). HUMAN→HUMAN axis — Lev 19:18 (neighbour-love commandment), Mat 5:44 (enemy-love), Joh 13:34 (new commandment), Joh 13:35 (community identity); these span the rea-group (173 active), the agapaō-mutual group (571-004, 53 relevant), and the filadelfia group (558-001). The evidential foundation confirms that all three axes have strong lexical and verse-level grounding, with the GOD→HUMAN axis carrying the greatest evidential weight.

Love primarily **enables and deepens** the perceptive faculty. It enables: love for God opens the person to perceive what others cannot — Hos 6:6 (OBS-103-091) pairs knowledge of God with chesed, implying love as the condition for spiritual perception of God's ways. Love deepens: Phili 4:8 (OBS-103-133) — the mind directed to attend to what is lovely (prosfilēs) — love sharpens the inner sense for what is worth perceiving. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): Jesus' love-disclosure opens the disciples' perception to what he has heard from the Father. Conversely, disordered love **impairs** perception: Luk 16:14 (OBS-103-136) — the Pharisees' love-of-money produces rejection of what they hear; Joh 3:19 (OBS-103-147) — love of darkness blinds to the light. The direction of love determines whether the perceptive faculty is opened or closed.

Love **deepens and intensifies** affect rather than bypassing or impairing it. The love-vocabulary's emotional range is the widest in the programme (OBS-103-051 — from deepest joy to deepest grief). Specific deepening evidence: Song 5:6 (OBS-103-062) — love's absence produces soul-failing, one of the most acute affective states in the corpus. 2Sa 1:26 (OBS-103-095) — love measured by its grief: 'your love was extraordinary, surpassing the love of women.' The intensity of love produces intensity of affect — not merely as a side-effect but as love's evidence and measure. Love also enables affect that would otherwise be inaccessible: love of enemy (Mat 5:44) enables compassion where enmity would produce contempt; love of God enables the reverential joy of Psa 63:3 ('better than life'). Disordered love distorts affect: love-of-money produces 'many pangs' (1Ti 6:10, OBS-103-132) — the wrong love generates the wrong affective outcomes.

Love **enables and expands** the volitional capacity. Evidence: (a) Capacity — love of God produces the capacity for commandment-keeping (Joh 14:15, OBS-103-140) — love gives the will the motivation it needs to sustain obedience. Without love, will-alone cannot sustain the commandment-pattern; with love, the will is equipped. (b) Interaction with other characteristics — love interacts with will positively: Mat 10:37 (OBS-103-151) — the will must choose which love ranks highest; this choice-act is love's volitional exercise. (c) Constraints — enemy-love (Mat 5:44, OBS-103-143) operates against the constraint of natural antipathy; love expands the volitional range by enabling choices that natural feeling would block. Disordered love **impairs** volition by narrowing its range: love-of-money produces craving (OBS-103-132) — the will is progressively constrained toward the money-object and away from faith-objects.

The volitional engagement pattern reveals that love and will are not rivals but partners in the data's inner-being architecture. Love is not opposed to will (as if love were pure feeling that will must constrain) — love provides the motivational ground that the will exercises from. This is the pattern of Joh 14:15: love of Christ produces the will's obedience, not as external compulsion but as the natural expression of the inner love. Enemy-love (Mat 5:44) pushes this furthest: love extends the will's reach beyond what it could achieve through discipline alone. The pattern also reveals that disordered love progressively constrains the will — the person addicted to a wrong love-object finds their volitional freedom increasingly reduced. Love's quality determines the will's freedom: righteous love expands volitional range; disordered love narrows it toward its object.

Love **enables and extends** agency beyond its natural range. The key evidence: enemy-love (Mat 5:44) produces agency toward those the person would naturally avoid — love extends the agentive reach to include hostile others. Joh 15:13 (OBS-103-125): love produces the maximum agentive act (laying down the soul) that no other motivation could reliably sustain. Ruth's chesed-love (Rut 3:10, OBS-103-089) produces the agentive choice against self-interest — love expands agency by overriding the self-protective constraints that would otherwise limit it. Disordered love produces distorted agency: love-of-money produces the agency of departure from faith (1Ti 6:10); love-of-primacy produces the agency of exclusion (3Jo 9, OBS-103-155). The direction of love determines the direction and quality of agency — love is the inner source from which the person acts outward.

Love **enables and calibrates** moral evaluation — it is the primary calibrator of the moral faculty's standards. Psa 97:10 (OBS-103-115): 'O you who love the Lord, hate evil' — love of God calibrates the moral evaluation that rightly identifies evil as what must be hated. Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114): the prohibition on hating the brother 'in your heart' requires moral evaluation of inner states, not just outer acts — love enables the inner moral evaluation that reaches below behaviour to motivation. 1Cor 13:6 (within OBS-103-127): love 'does not rejoice in wrongdoing but rejoices with the truth' — love calibrates the moral evaluation faculty so that wrongdoing generates the correct response (non-rejoicing) and truth generates joy. Disordered love impairs moral evaluation: the Pharisees' love-of-money produced mockery of Jesus' teaching (Luk 16:14, OBS-103-136) — a distorted moral evaluation produced by a distorted love.

The moral evaluation pattern reveals love as the moral faculty's primary standard-source. The person calibrated by love-of-God sees evil as evil and good as good (Psa 97:10) — the love-orientation determines the correctness of the moral evaluation. This means moral failure is often misdiagnosed: what looks like impaired moral faculty may be a disordered love-faculty — the person who evaluates wrongly does so because they love wrongly (Luk 16:14 — money-love produces moral mockery). The pattern implies that moral formation cannot proceed without love-formation: sharpening the moral faculty without reordering the love-faculty produces moralists who identify evil correctly but love wrongly. The data's architecture places love prior to moral evaluation: love → moral evaluation → right action, not moral evaluation → motivation → action. Love calibrates the evaluator before the evaluation occurs.

From the available evidence: love appears to **enable** conscience by providing the standard against which conscience operates. Lev 19:17 (OBS-103-114) — 'do not hate your brother in your heart' — implies conscience is the faculty that registers the inner hate as moral failure; love is what conscience is protecting on behalf of. The conscience that has internalised love's requirements is the conscience that registers hate-in-the-heart as a violation. Conversely, disordered love may **impair** conscience: the money-lover (Luk 16:14, OBS-103-136) who ridicules Jesus' teaching has a conscience whose sensitivity to error is dulled by the competing love-object. Gap: the direct conscience-love relationship is inferred from indirect evidence. The suneidēsis vocabulary does not appear in R103's anchor verses. Partial answer.

The indirect pattern suggests love and conscience are functionally complementary but operate at different constitutional levels. Love provides the positive orientation (toward the beloved, toward the good); conscience provides the negative monitor (detecting violations of that orientation). Together they constitute a complete moral inner-being system: love sets the direction; conscience enforces the boundaries. If this is accurate, love is what conscience is oriented to protect — the conscience registers violations of love's requirements (hate-in-the-heart, false-brotherhood, betrayal-kiss). Gap: the data cannot confirm this functional complementarity directly from named evidence. It is an inference from the structural juxtaposition of Lev 19:17 (hate-prohibition) and Lev 19:18 (love-command). Session D treatment required for confirmed resolution.

pointers_orphan.label: DIM-42-SD004
pointers_orphan.text: Group 3090-001 (hēdonē) occupies a counter-position to the positive delight vocabulary. The pleasure/delight tension in the NT — where hēdonē names the adversary of the ordered inner life — is a significant cross-registry pattern. Session D should examine the structured opposition between competing inner orientations as a programme-level finding.

pointers_orphan.label: DIM-52-SD001
pointers_orphan.text: C18 produces 9 groups assigned Divine-Human Correspondence across division (5), rejection (1), strife (1), and unity (2) — 20% of the cluster. This is the first systematic appearance of dimension 11 in the programme. Session D should examine whether Divine-Human Correspondence groups cluster around particular semantic domains (belonging/portion, rejection, abundance) or whether they represent a cross-domain phenomenon. The C18 data suggests that relational vocabulary — division, unity, belonging — is intrinsically prone to divine-human correspondence structure.

pointers_orphan.label: DIM-58-SD001
pointers_orphan.text: Reg 58 (experience) is dominated by property terms (ra.ah = to see/appear; energeō/energēma/energēs = to work/operative). The ten ra.ah groups serve eight distinct inner-being dimensions — Cognition, Emotion — Negative, Moral Character, Transformation, Dependence/Creatureliness, Theological/Divine-Human, and Agency/Power — demonstrating that a single property term can serve the full range of inner-being characteristics depending on context. The energeō family covers Agency/Power in three registers: divine, hostile, and inner-being forces. Session D should assess: (a) whether ra.ah and energeō belong in the same registry; (b) whether experience is a valid inner-being category or an English-word artefact; (c) how the variety of inner-being registers accessed by a single property term should be handled in cross-registry synthesis.

pointers_orphan.label: apeitheō (G0544) connects registry 165 unbelief to registry 50 disobedient
pointers_orphan.text: Cross-registry pointer raised during vcb-010 verse-context re-evaluation of apeitheō (G0544, mti=1199) in registry 165 (unbelief). The Session A XREF table for this term lists G0545 apeithēs (disobedient) as OWNER in registry 50. STEP/Mounce: the verb does not separate 'disbelieve' and 'disobey' the way English does — the same verb expresses both. Five characteristic-distinct groups emerged in vcb-010: two clusters around refusal-of-Christ-revelation and active-hostility (1199-001, 1199-002); three around moral-truth-refusal, covenantal-disobedience, and Pauline-mercy-logic-disobedience (1199-003, 1199-004, 1199-005). Session D implication: the synthesis between registry 165 (unbelief) and registry 50 (disobedient) needs an explicit account of how the same Greek verb is distributed across two semantic-domain registries — and whether the registry-50 OWNER analysis of G0545 apeithēs informs (or is informed by) the registry-165 treatment of G0544 apeitheō. The five-group structure for G0544 in 165 already exposes the two halves of the verb's semantic range — Session D may consider this when integrating the two registries' findings.

pointers_paired_keyword.label: DIM-146-SD001
pointers_paired_keyword.text: Registry 146 (shame) and Registry 190 (contempt) are relational inverses in C06: contempt is the inner disposition that assigns worthlessness; shame is the inner experience of being assigned worthlessness. The honour/shame axis linking these two registries is a cross-cluster synthesis question for Session D. Both registries share Relational/Social and Theological/Divine-Human as significant dimensions.

pointers_paired_keyword.label: DIM-75-SD001
pointers_paired_keyword.text: Group 902-003 (sin.ah: hatred listed alongside love and envy as constitutive inner orientations of the person that cease at death) makes an anthropological claim: hatred is a fundamental inner capacity defining relational existence. Session D should examine what Scripture claims about the constitutive inner orientations of the human person — whether love, hatred, and envy form a structured set of relational capacities, and what their cessation at death implies for the inner being's post-mortem state.

pointers_paired_keyword.label: DIM-001-SD001
pointers_paired_keyword.text: The new covenant transformation group (3272-001) appears in the abomination registry and salvation vocabulary groups appear in the debauchery registry (Reg 39) — both placing redemptive vocabulary within moral-failure/defilement registries through the XREF mechanism. Session D should examine whether this is a programme-wide pattern: are there other clusters where redemptive/salvation vocabulary appears in moral-failure registries via XREF, and what does this reveal about the inner-being logic connecting defilement and redemption in Scripture?

pointers_paired_keyword.label: DIM-023-SD005
pointers_paired_keyword.text:
Isa 63:9 names ahavah (love) and chem.lah (pity/compassion) together as co-motivating God's redemption. This is a direct verse-level co-occurrence of Love (Reg 103) and Compassion (Reg 23) vocabularies within a single clause about divine inner motivation for salvation. Raise: the inner architecture of divine redemption — what roles do love and compassion play distinctly, and do they always appear together? Cross-registry: Love (Reg 103), shared anchor verse candidate.
